questions.question: What is the biggest early mistake in a messaging-platform interview?
options: Starting architecture before clarifying semantics and guarantee boundaries.
Drawing boxes on a whiteboard too neatly.
Estimating throughput before naming a cloud provider.
Choosing Go for helper code.
questions.explanation: Guarantees drive the whole rest of the design.

questions.question: Why should sizing include retry or replay amplification rather than only steady-state traffic?
options: Incident behavior can dominate the real load and expose weak designs.
Because steady-state traffic is never useful.
Because retries only matter in workflow systems.
Because amplification replaces the need for storage estimates.
questions.explanation: Many messaging incidents are failure-mode scale problems.

questions.question: Which deep dive is usually strongest for a distributed queue prompt?
options: Partitioning, lag recovery, and consumer progress tracking.
The color palette of the operator dashboard.
How to name every cloud resource uniquely.
An unrelated CDN caching strategy.
questions.explanation: The best deep dive usually attacks the core guarantee and scaling pressure.

questions.question: Why is a bounded guarantee often stronger than a broad absolute claim in interview design?
options: Because it is more accurate, defensible, and easier to explain under failure follow-ups.
Because interviewers dislike precise language.
Because bounded guarantees eliminate all trade-offs.
Because absolute claims are simpler to implement.
questions.explanation: Senior answers optimize for correctness and clarity, not hype.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with adding rich operator controls such as replay, pause, and seek?
options: You gain recoverability and auditability but increase control-plane complexity and risk.
You guarantee zero incidents forever.
You remove the need for observability.
You make throughput scaling automatic.
questions.explanation: Operational power needs careful design and guardrails.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that a candidate handled failure analysis well in the drill?
options: Each major failure includes both a detection metric and a mitigation path.
They used many product-brand names.
They skipped observability to save time.
They answered every follow-up with 'add more servers.'
questions.explanation: Concrete detection and response is the key quality bar.

questions.question: Why should redesign after a changed requirement alter a specific component or policy?
options: Because otherwise the answer sounds memorized rather than causally reasoned.
Because redesign is only for low-level coding interviews.
Because changed constraints should never affect architecture.
Because policy changes are always weaker than hardware changes.
questions.explanation: Good redesign answers show architectural consequences.

questions.question: What design change most improves a shared messaging-platform answer when one tenant becomes extremely noisy?
options: Add quotas, isolation lanes, or tiered capacity instead of treating all tenants identically.
Remove retry tracking to reduce control-plane state.
Disable replay for every tenant permanently.
Force all traffic into a single global partition.
questions.explanation: Shared platforms need explicit noisy-neighbor controls.
